1: Proverbs of Solomon (10:1) — a new collection heading; distinguishes this antithetical-couplet corpus from the discourses of chs 1–9. The bēn ḥākām / bēn kĕsîl (wise son / fool son) contrast sets the collection's dominant register: wisdom is relational and has social consequences, beginning in the home.
2: ṣĕdāqāh tassîl mimmāwet — “righteousness delivers from death.” Ṣĕdāqāh (H6666) has a semantic range from judicial rectitude to generosity/almsgiving; in rabbinic Hebrew it narrowed to charity. The MKT renders “righteousness” to preserve the forensic and moral range. “Delivers from death” (tassîl mimmāwet) is the Proverbs life/death binary: wisdom-aligned conduct leads away from the trajectory of Sheol.
3: lōʾ-yaʿăzōb YHWH nepeš ṣaddîq — “the LORD does not let the righteous go hungry.” Literally, “YHWH will not let the soul of the righteous go hungry (rāʿāb).” Nepeš is frequently rendered “soul” but here functions as the vitalizing appetite of the whole person. Hāwat rĕšāʿîm (the craving of the wicked) uses hawwāh — desire bordering on greed — which YHWH actively frustrates.
4: rāš ʿōśê kap rĕmiyyāh — a slack (rĕmiyyāh) hand brings poverty. Rĕmiyyāh can mean both “slack/lazy” and “deceitful/treacherous” — the same word used of the false balance in 11:1. The pun is live: slackness is a form of deception, pretending to work.
5: ʿōśê māśkîl — “a wise son gathers in summer” (lit. “acts wisely”). The agricultural image (qāṣîr, harvest) grounds wisdom in observable seasonal reality — there is a right time for action. Mēbîš (causes shame) is the consistent antonym of wisdom in Proverbs: shame is the social consequence of the fool’s failure to read time and circumstances.
6: bĕrākôt lĕrōʾš ṣaddîq — “blessings crown the head of the righteous.” Bĕrākāh (blessing) flows naturally to the righteous person; the chiastic second half — “the mouth of the wicked conceals violence (ḥāmās)” — contrasts blessings on the head with violence hidden in the mouth. Words both reveal and conceal character.
7: zēker ṣaddîq liḇrākāh — “the memory of the righteous is a blessing.” The verse affirms that moral reputation outlasts life itself: the righteous leave a name that blesses posterity, while the wicked’s name rots (yirqāb). This is not immortality but šēm-honor — the blessing of being remembered well.
8: ḥakam-lēb yiqqaḥ miṣwôt — “the wise in heart (lēb) accepts commands.” Lēb (heart/mind) is the Proverbs seat of will and understanding together; wisdom is not abstract intelligence but integrated character. Yiqqaḥ miṣwôt (receives commands) contrasts with the babbling fool (ʾĕwîl śĕpātayim) who talks rather than heeds.
9: hôlēk battōm yēlēk beṭaḥ — “whoever walks in integrity (tōm) walks securely (beṭaḥ).” Tōm (integrity/completeness) is used of complete fidelity in covenant contexts. Beṭaḥ (security, trust) is the condition of the one whose path is not crooked; the double-walk (hôlēk... yēlēk) emphasizes that integrity and security travel together.
10: qōrēṣ ʿāyin — “the one who winks with malice.” The eye-wink (qāraṣ ʿayin) signals deceptive intent (cf. 6:13); it communicates what is not said aloud, making the winking person a disruptor of communal trust. The fool who babbles (repeated from v8) disrupts trust differently — with too many words rather than too few.
11: maqqôr ḥayyîm — “fountain of life.” The mouth-as-fountain image is central to Proverbs’ speech theology: the righteous person’s words are life-giving like a spring. The image recurs at 13:14; 14:27; 16:22 (each time as maqqôr ḥayyîm). The wicked’s mouth, by contrast, “conceals violence (ḥāmās)” — the same word used for pre-flood human wickedness (Gen 6:11).
12: ḥesed tĕkassē kol-pĕšāʿîm — “love covers all offenses.” Ḥesed (H2617; dispute_level=4) is one of the most theologically loaded Hebrew words: covenant loyalty, steadfast love, mercy. The MKT renders “love” here to capture both the relational and affective dimensions. The verse is quoted directly in 1 Pet 4:8, applied to fervent love among believers. The covering of offenses is not blind indifference but actively absorbing conflict rather than inflaming it.
13: ḥokmāh... śĕbĕṭ lĕgēw ḥăsar-lēb — wisdom on the lips of the discerning; a rod for the back of those who lack lēb (heart/understanding). The rod is not punitive cruelty but the corrective consequence designed to produce the understanding the fool refuses to acquire through instruction. Wisdom is the default state of the discerning; corporal correction is the fallback.
14: ḥăkāmîm yiṣpĕnû dāʿat — “wise people store up knowledge.” Ṣāpan (store, hide) — the same verb used for Moses’ mother hiding him (Exod 2:2) — suggests that knowledge is a strategic reserve, not for display. The fool’s mouth, by contrast, is mĕḥittāh qĕrōbāh (destruction near): voluble ignorance brings harm down quickly.
15: hôn ʿāšîr qiryat ʿuzzô — “a wealthy person’s riches are like a fortified city (qiryat ʿuzzô).” The verse observes social reality without endorsing it: wealth functions as protection, while poverty is the poor man’s ruin. The observation is later complicated by v22 (the blessing of YHWH makes truly rich) and 11:4 (wealth is useless on the day of wrath).
16: pĕʿullat ṣaddîq lĕḥayyîm — “the earnings of the righteous lead to life (ḥayyîm).” The ṣaddîq / rāšāʿ binary extends into economics: the righteous person’s labor produces life, while the wicked’s income (tĕbûʾat rāšāʿ) leads to sin. The point is not that poverty is sinful but that money is morally directional.
17: ʾōraḥ lĕḥayyîm šômēr mûsār — “whoever keeps instruction (mûsār) is on the path of life (ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm).” Mûsār is disciplinary instruction (from yāsar, to chastise/correct) — formation through correction. The path-of-life formula (also 6:23; Ps 16:11) is a major organizing metaphor of wisdom: life is a road, and attending to correction keeps the traveler on the right one.
18: mĕkassē śinaʾāh śiprê šāqer — “whoever conceals hatred has lying lips.” The verse probes the relationship between speech and inner reality: the person who hides hatred through polished speech is a liar by definition. Śinaʾāh (hatred) beneath smooth words is the precise dynamics of hypocrisy; the second line compounds it with slander (môṣîʾ dibbāh — spreading an evil report).
19: bĕrōb dĕbārîm lōʾ-yĕḥdal pāšaʿ — “when words are many, sin is not absent.” One of Proverbs’ most concise speech-ethics axioms. Ḥōśēk śĕpātāyw maskîl — “whoever restrains his lips is prudent.” Ḥāśak (restrain, hold back) is active verbal discipline. The proverb captures a consistent wisdom insight: more words = more opportunity for sin; restraint = wisdom.
20: kesep niḇḥār lĕšôn ṣaddîq — “the tongue of the righteous is like choice silver.” The tongue-as-precious-metal image measures the value of righteous speech: rare, refined, and valuable. The heart of the wicked, by contrast, has kimʿāṭ (little value). The contrast is between refined utterance and worthless interior disposition.
21: śiptê ṣaddîq yirʿû rabbîm — “the lips of the righteous feed many.” Rāʿāh (to feed/shepherd) extends the agricultural/pastoral register: righteous speech is pastoral, sustaining a community. Fools, by contrast, die (yāmûtû) for lack of heart (lēb). The inversion is sharp: the righteous nourish others while fools cannot even sustain themselves.
22: bĕrakat YHWH hîʾ taʿăšîr — “the blessing of YHWH is what truly makes rich.” The adverb hîʾ (it is this that) places the emphasis: not diligence alone, not strategy, but YHWH’s blessing. Wĕlōʾ-yôsip ʿeṣeb ʿimmāh (he adds no sorrow to it) — the blessing of YHWH comes without the grief that accompanies wealth gained by other means (cf. v15, where riches without YHWH’s blessing are a fragile fortress).
23: kimśĕḥōq lĕkĕsîl ʿăśôt zimmāh — “doing wrong is like a game to a fool.” Zimmāh (wicked scheme/lewdness) is treated as entertainment (śĕḥōq, laughter) by the fool. The serious moral weight of evil is invisible to those without wisdom. By contrast, wisdom (ḥokmāh) is the śimḥāh (delight) of the person of understanding — their genuine pleasure.
24: megôrat rāšāʿ hîʾ tĕbôʾennû — “what the wicked fears will come upon him.” The self-fulfilling nature of the wicked person’s trajectory: their megôrāh (dread, the thing dreaded) comes to them. The contrast with the righteous whose longing (taʾăwat ṣaddîqîm) is granted positions desire and dread as directional: you receive what you are oriented toward.
25: kĕʿābōr sûpāh wĕʾên rāšāʿ wĕṣaddîq yĕsôd ʿôlam — “when the storm passes, the wicked are gone; the righteous stand on an everlasting foundation (yĕsôd ʿôlam).” The storm metaphor is eschatological in Proverbs: ultimate stability belongs to the righteous because their foundation is ʿôlam (eternal/lasting). The wicked have no such anchor; they are swept away.
26: kĕḥōmeṣ lĕšinnayim ûkĕʿāšān lĕʿênāyim — “like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes.” The uncomfortable sensory images (acid on teeth, irritating smoke) capture the frustration of relying on a lazy person. The proverb uses physical discomfort as the experiential equivalent of the social cost of laziness.
27: yirʾat YHWH tôsîp yāmîm — “the fear of YHWH (yirʾat YHWH) adds length to life.” The fear-of-YHWH formula is Proverbs’ epistemological anchor (1:7; 9:10); here it has a directly temporal consequence: added days. The wicked’s years are yiqṣōrû (cut short) — the life/death binary expressed in longevity terms.
28: tôḥelet ṣaddîqîm śimḥāh — “the hope of the righteous brings joy.” Tôḥelet (hope, expectation) in Proverbs is not wishful thinking but confident anticipation rooted in YHWH’s ordering of the world. The wicked’s expectation (tiqwat rĕšāʿîm) comes to nothing (tōʾbad) — not because hope is wrong but because their hope is misplaced.
29: māʿôz lattōm derek YHWH — “the LORD’s way (derek YHWH) is a stronghold (māʿôz) for the blameless.” The divine way — YHWH’s path of governance — is simultaneously shelter for those who walk it and ruin (mĕḥittāh) for those who work evil. The same divine order that protects the righteous destroys the wicked.
30: ṣaddîq lĕʿôlam bal-yimmôṭ — “the righteous will never be dislodged (yimmôṭ).” Mōṭ (to totter, slip, be dislodged) is used of the unstable; the righteous have secure footing (lĕʿôlam, forever). The wicked will not inhabit the land (ereṣ) — reversing the covenant promise; evil disrupts the conditions of the covenantal land.
31: pî ṣaddîq yānûb ḥokmāh — “the mouth of the righteous produces wisdom.” Nûb (to bear fruit) is an agricultural verb applied to speech — the mouth of the righteous is a fruit-bearing plant. The perverse tongue (tahpûkôt) will be cut off (tikārēt). The speech-plant metaphor that ends ch10 anticipates 11:30’s “fruit of the righteous is a tree of life.”
32: śiptê ṣaddîq yēdĕʿûn rāṣôn — “the lips of the righteous know what is pleasing (rāṣôn).” Rāṣôn (pleasure, favor, delight) implies social and theological attunement: the righteous person’s speech is pleasing to YHWH and to people because it is rightly calibrated. The wicked’s mouth speaks only tahpûkôt (perversities, turnings-upside-down).
